unit.name: Neural-network modules and the training loop
unit.description: Shows how to define layers with torch.nn, compose them into blocks and custom modules, inspect parameters, choose losses and optimizers, and implement a full PyTorch training loop.
unit.summary: This final segment moves from tensors to neural-network construction. It introduces torch.nn and explains nn.Linear as a layer parameterized by input and output dimensions that maps the last dimension of its input to the specified output size, effectively acting like an affine transformation with optional bias [ts=2098s] [ts=2163s]. A worked example passes a 2x3x4 tensor through a linear layer with output size 2 and obtains a 2x3x2 tensor; a mismatched input dimension example is used to argue again for printing shapes when debugging layer errors [ts=2183s] [ts=2286s]. The instructor shows that model parameters, including weights and bias, can be inspected and that they store gradients to be updated during training [ts=2383s] [ts=2419s]. Other module building blocks are briefly surveyed, including convolutions, batch normalization, upsampling, and pooling [ts=2424s] [ts=2451s]. The segment then adds nonlinearities with sigmoid and uses nn.Sequential to compose a linear layer and activation into a reusable block [ts=2474s] [ts=2528s]. A custom multilayer perceptron is implemented by subclassing nn.Module, defining parameters in **init**, and defining the computation in forward [ts=2531s] [ts=2632s]. Finally, optimization is introduced through torch.optim: the model, Adam optimizer, and cross-entropy loss are instantiated, and the canonical training loop is demonstrated as zero_grad, forward pass, loss computation, backward, and optimizer step [ts=2645s] [ts=2762s]. The segment ends by emphasizing that PyTorch abstracts away manual backpropagation and optimizer details so students can focus on assembling architectures from reusable components [ts=2769s] [ts=2813s].

key_points.text: nn.Linear takes an input dimension and output dimension and transforms the last input dimension to the output size, with an optional bias term.
Passing a 2x3x4 tensor through a linear layer with output size 2 yields a 2x3x2 output tensor.
Printing shapes is an effective way to debug layer mismatch errors because internal error messages may flatten or reinterpret tensor shapes.
Neural-network parameters include both weights and bias and are accessible for inspection through the layer's parameters.
nn.Sequential can compose layers such as a linear transform followed by sigmoid into a single callable block.
To define a custom network in PyTorch, subclass nn.Module, initialize components in **init**, and implement the forward pass in forward.
A standard training loop is: optimizer.zero_grad(), predictions = model(x), compute loss, loss.backward(), optimizer.step().

A worked example passes a 2x3x4 tensor through a linear layer with output size 2 and obtains a 2x3x2 tensor; a mismatched input dimension example is used to argue again for printing shapes when debugging layer errors [ts=2183s] [ts=2286s]. The instructor shows that model parameters, including weights and bias, can be inspected and that they store gradients to be updated during training [ts=2383s] [ts=2419s]. Finally, optimization is introduced through torch.optim: the model, Adam optimizer, and cross-entropy loss are instantiated, and the canonical training loop is demonstrated as zero_grad, forward pass, loss computation, backward, and optimizer step [ts=2645s] [ts=2762s]. The segment ends by emphasizing that PyTorch abstracts away manual backpropagation and optimizer details so students can focus on assembling architectures from reusable components [ts=2769s] [ts=2813s].

kp_catalog.name: Neural-network modules and composition
kp_catalog.description: PyTorch provides reusable modules such as linear layers, activations, and Sequential blocks, and custom networks are created by subclassing nn.Module.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because neural-network modules and composition formalizes how pyTorch provides reusable modules such as linear layers, activations, and Sequential blocks, and custom networks are created by subclassing nn.Module, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: PyTorch training loop
kp_catalog.description: A standard PyTorch training loop combines forward computation, loss evaluation, backward gradient computation, and an optimizer step inside an epoch loop. Treating Hugging Face models as standard PyTorch modules inside a DataLoader-based training loop with optimizer and scheduler.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because pyTorch training loop synthesizes a standard PyTorch training loop combines forward computation, loss evaluation, backward gradient computation, and an optimizer step inside an epoch loop into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.

kp_catalog.name: Shape-based debugging
kp_catalog.description: Printing and checking tensor shapes is a core debugging habit for understanding errors and verifying that operations behave as intended.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because shape-based debugging clarifies printing and checking tensor shapes is a core debugging habit for understanding errors and verifying that operations behave as intended, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Tensor shapes and broadcasting
kp_catalog.description: Reasoning about tensor dimensions, shape compatibility, and broadcasting is necessary for elementwise operations, batching, and matrix multiplication. Core shape vocabulary for rectangular matrices, one-dimensional vectors, higher-dimensional tensors, and NumPy shape notation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because tensor shapes and broadcasting formalizes how reasoning about tensor dimensions, shape compatibility, and broadcasting is necessary for elementwise operations, batching, and matrix multiplication, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context though importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Computational graphs and automatic differentiation
kp_catalog.description: General reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation. A neural network can be understood as a directed acyclic graph of operations whose forward pass computes values and whose backward pass computes gradients.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because computational graphs and automatic differentiation formalizes how general reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Gradient accumulation and zero_grad
kp_catalog.description: PyTorch accumulates gradients across backward passes, so training code must explicitly zero them before each new optimization step.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gradient accumulation and zero_grad clarifies pyTorch accumulates gradients across backward passes, so training code must explicitly zero them before each new optimization step, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
